items.evidence_text:
Advisor: Got it—so $0 in monthly debt payments. Let me jot that down—one sec.
Advisor: Now, income. Roughly what’s coming in over a year, gross, for the household—before taxes?
Client 1: Between the pension and the rental, it’s about $168,000 a year gross.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to $160,000.
Client 1: No, Michael—your number is net-ish. Gross is about $167,950. The rental swings, but that’s the annualized figure we’ve been using.

Advisor: Great. Going back to what you said, Michael—spending. About what do your monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: Uh, like six grand a month? Give or take.
Client 1: It’s a little under that—about $5,900 a month. I have it written down.
Advisor: Perfect—$5,900 per month. And when you say expenses, you mean your living costs, not including any debt payments, right?
Client 2: Wait—does that include the credit card bill? Because we pay that every month.

Advisor: Great. Going back to what you said, Michael—spending. About what do your monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: Uh, like six grand a month? Give or take.
Client 1: It’s a little under that—about $5,900 a month. I have it written down.
Advisor: Perfect—$5,900 per month. And when you say expenses, you mean your living costs, not including any debt payments, right?
Client 2: Wait—does that include the credit card bill? Because we pay that every month.

Advisor: I hear you. We’ll come back to taxes in a bit. For now, assets—ballpark, how much do you have invested that’s accessible, not counting the house?
Client 2: A million? Maybe a bit more.
Client 1: It’s more like $1.15 million—$1,154,200 total investable, last time I totaled it.
Advisor: Okay—about $1,154,200 in investable assets. When you say “totaled it,” what buckets make that up?
Client 1: There’s a joint taxable account—about $470,900. It’s at a bank.

Advisor: Great—$46,800 private markets via a bank platform.
Advisor: And then the home—Michael, is the primary residence in your name?
Client 2: Yeah, it’s mine. Value is about $688,550.
Client 1: And no loan on it. Just to be clear.
Advisor: Perfect—primary residence about $688,550, and no outstanding loan. So liabilities overall are basically none, correct?

Client 2: Wait—does that include the credit card bill? Because we pay that every month.

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi Michael—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. I’m Margaret. We’re retired, and I just want a clean plan—income, taxes, and making sure we’re not missing anything.
Client 2: Yeah, and I’m Michael. I mostly want to know, like, are we okay to keep spending what we spend without stressing about it.
Advisor: Totally fair. And just so I’m labeling things right—Margaret, you’re Client 1, and Michael, you’re Client 2. Correct?

Client 1: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Alright—thanks. Going back to that earlier point about the house and no mortgage, that helps a lot.
Advisor: I want to zoom out for a second and make sure I’ve got the household basics right. You two are married, correct—Margaret and Michael?
Client 2: Yeah, married.
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.

Advisor: Great. Going back to what you said, Michael—spending. About what do your monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: Uh, like six grand a month? Give or take.
Client 1: It’s a little under that—about $5,900 a month. I have it written down.
Advisor: Perfect—$5,900 per month. And when you say expenses, you mean your living costs, not including any debt payments, right?
Client 2: Wait—does that include the credit card bill? Because we pay that every month.

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi Michael—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. I’m Margaret. We’re retired, and I just want a clean plan—income, taxes, and making sure we’re not missing anything.
Client 2: Yeah, and I’m Michael. I mostly want to know, like, are we okay to keep spending what we spend without stressing about it.

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi Michael—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. I’m Margaret. We’re retired, and I just want a clean plan—income, taxes, and making sure we’re not missing anything.
Client 2: Yeah, and I’m Michael. I mostly want to know, like, are we okay to keep spending what we spend without stressing about it.
Advisor: Totally fair. And just so I’m labeling things right—Margaret, you’re Client 1, and Michael, you’re Client 2. Correct?
Client 1: Yes, I’m Client 1.

Client 1: Right, just the two of us.
Advisor: Great, two adults. Let me note that down—hang on.
Advisor: Next one is more “industry label” than anything. Are you working with me in a U.S. registered investment advisor setup—so, US RIA—versus like a bank brokerage channel?
Client 2: Uh, I thought you were, like, a broker?
Client 1: No, Michael—remember, he’s the RIA. Fee-based, not commissions.

Client 2: Right—sorry. Yes, US RIA. I mix those up.
Advisor: Totally get it—people use “broker” as a generic word. So we’ll mark the market as US RIA.
Advisor: Now, risk tolerance—earlier you both sounded kind of middle-of-the-road. Margaret, you want guardrails; Michael, you’re a bit more okay with swings. If you had to pick a single label, is it moderate?
Client 2: I’d say moderate, yeah.
Client 1: Moderate. Not conservative, but not aggressive either.

Client 1: Moderate. Not conservative, but not aggressive either.
Advisor: Perfect—moderate risk tolerance.
Advisor: And then taxes—this is where people sometimes answer with what they paid last year versus their bracket. When you think about your federal bracket, are you around the 24% bracket?
Client 2: Is that like… the percent we pay on everything? Because it doesn’t feel like 24% on the whole amount.
Advisor: Great question—no, it’s not on every dollar. It’s the marginal bracket on the top slice of income. So, bracket-wise, do you land in the 24% band?

Advisor: Perfect, thank you both. Michael, I know we already talked household income at a high level, but can we zoom in on your piece for a second—what do you show as your gross annual income?
Client 2: Uh, I thought it was like seventy-five thousand?
Client 1: It’s not 75—hang on—it's $72,250 for Michael. I have it written down.
Client 2: Yeah—sorry, $72,250 gross for me. Margaret’s right.
Advisor: Got it—$72,250 gross annually for you, Michael. Thanks for catching that, Margaret.

Client 1: Busy, but good. Okay—yeah.
Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I hear you, Margaret. Okay, going back to what you said earlier about the rental being in your name, I want to make sure I’ve got each of you labeled correctly in my notes.
Advisor: Michael, just to confirm, you’re Client 2 and your role in the household is spouse/partner, right?
Client 2: Yeah, I’m Client 2—spouse/partner.
Client 1: And I’m Client 1, just so we’re consistent.

Client 2: Retired. Yeah, no work income.
Advisor: Okay—retired. And then one more admin detail, Michael—your date of birth? Month, day, year is fine.
Client 2: December 6th, 1965.
Advisor: December 6, 1965—thank you.
Client 1: That’s right.
